title: practical english usage - Part 268
content:

British English, and more often with full stops (Mr., Mrs., Dr.) in American Abbreviated titles like Mr, Mrs and Dr are generally written without full stops in 3  notes     on   titles and West) when addressing people, both formally and informally.

In American English, sir and ma'am are quite often used (especially in the South not Excuse me. Can you tell me the time? (     Excuse mer sir. ..) situations sir and madam are unusual in British English.

of beginning letters to strangers (►   289) - note the capital letters. ‘ incorrect', but it has been common in educated speech for centuries.

somebody, anybody, nobody, person. This usage is sometimes considered They is often used to mean ‘ he or she', especially after indefinite words like 3  singular      they If a student is ill, he or she must send a medical certificate...

alternatives, especially in a formal style.

him  or her and his or her (also he/she, him/her, his/her) are common This usage is now widely regarded as sexist, and is generally avoided. He or she, If a student is ill, he must send his medical certificate to the College office.

If I ever find the person who did that, I'll kill him.

a formal style.

or in references that could apply to either men or women, especially in Traditionally, English used he/him/his when the sex of a person was not known, 2 he   or   she Norway has decided to increase its trade with Romania. (OR ... her trade...) We can use she for countries, but it is more common in modern English.

The ship's struck a rock. She's sinking!

‘ How's your new car? ‘ Terrific. She's running beautifully.' ships (but most other people use it).

Some people use she for cars, motorbikes, etc; sailors often use she for boats and She had an old dog who always slept in her bed.

In these cases, who is often used instead of which.

Go and find the cat and put her out.

Once upon a time there was a rabbit called Joe. He lived.. .

domestic animals like cats, dogs and horses.

having personality, intelligence or feelings. This is common with pets and People sometimes call animals he or she, especially when they are thought of as 1 animals,      cars,   ships    and   countries are he or she and things are it. Note the following points.

instance, Allemagne, Tyskland or Niemcy). humanity or the human  race. Note also the common use of synthetic instead This is now generally considered sexist, and preferred terms are people, on stepping onto the moon) That's one small step for a man, one giant leap for mankind. (Neil Armstrong, Why does man have more diseases than animals?

Man and mankind have traditionally been used for the human race.
